culture.detail: The casino requires smart casual attire in the evening, with jackets preferred for men in certain gaming areas; bring your passport for entry.
Estoril beaches are family-friendly and relatively formal compared to other Portuguese resorts; toplessness is accepted but full nudity is not.
Dinner service begins late (around 20:00), and upscale restaurants expect reservations; lunch typically runs 12:30–15:00.
Shops and some services close between 13:00–15:00 for lunch; plan activities accordingly and embrace the leisurely afternoon pace.

places.name: Monsanto Forest Park
places.description: Lisbon's largest green space with 1000 acres of forest, hiking trails, and panoramic viewpoints overlooking the city and Tagus River. A peaceful escape from urban hustle offering nature walks and spectacular sunset vistas.

activities.detail: Explore over 7 kilometers of well-marked trails winding through pine and eucalyptus forests with varying difficulty levels suitable for all fitness levels.

activities.text: Sunset Viewing at Cristo Rei Viewpoint
activities.detail: Walk to the elevated panoramic spot offering sweeping views of Lisbon's skyline, the Cristo Rei monument across the Tagus, and the 25 de Abril Bridge at golden hour.
Discover a curated collection of native Portuguese plants and Mediterranean species arranged in themed garden sections throughout the forest park.
Use the park's scattered picnic zones equipped with benches and tables to enjoy traditional Portuguese pastries and local cheese while surrounded by forest.
The forest hosts various bird species including buzzards and sparrowhawks, making it ideal for ornithologists and photographers seeking natural Lisbon wildlife.
Rent a bike from nearby shops and traverse the gentler, wider trails that accommodate cyclists while maintaining the peaceful natural atmosphere.

food.detail: Seek out this iconic Portuguese custard tart with crispy pastry from the cafés near the park entrance, perfect for a quick snack before hiking.
This creamy, soft sheep's cheese from the nearby Serra region pairs excellently with bread and fruit for a park picnic experience.
A traditional Portuguese combination often found in nearby local restaurants, featuring fresh grilled sardines served with dense, hearty cornbread.

food.text: Fresh Orange Juice from Park Vendors
food.detail: Local vendors throughout Monsanto offer freshly squeezed orange and citrus juices ideal for hydration during afternoon walks.

culture.detail: Portuguese locals visit Monsanto primarily on weekends and late afternoons, so early morning visits guarantee a more peaceful, authentic forest experience away from crowds.
The park operates with an implicit cultural expectation of minimal noise and disturbance; keep music off, speak softly, and stay on marked trails to honor the space.

culture.text: Dress in Layers for Variable Weather
culture.detail: The elevation and forest canopy create microclimates; locals always bring light jackets and extra layers even on warm Lisbon days for comfort throughout your visit.
Most Portuguese visitors arrive via Lisbon's tram and bus system rather than driving; parking is limited and locals prefer the tram journey as part of the outing experience.

places.name: Alfama District
places.description: Lisbon's oldest and most atmospheric neighborhood, a maze of narrow medieval streets cascading down to the Tagus with traditional fado music venues and historic charm. Climb to São Jorge Castle for stunning city views and wander the authentic Portuguese quarters.

activities.detail: Climb to this 11th-century fortress perched above Alfama for panoramic views of the Tagus River and the entire Lisbon skyline.
Experience an intimate fado performance in a traditional restaurant where local singers perform melancholic Portuguese songs with raw emotion.
Get lost in the labyrinth of narrow medieval streets, arched passageways, and hidden squares that define the neighborhood's authentic character.
Visit Lisbon's stunning Romanesque cathedral with its fortress-like towers and beautifully preserved interior near the heart of Alfama.

activities.text: Miradouro da Senhora do Monte Viewpoint
activities.detail: Hike to this elevated terrace for breathtaking sunset views over Alfama, the Tagus, and the Cristo Rei monument across the river.
Stroll along the waterfront promenade at the base of Alfama where traditional fishing boats and riverside restaurants offer authentic atmosphere.

food.detail: A rich seafood rice dish cooked with local Tagus catch, saffron, and white wine that showcases the neighborhood's maritime heritage.
Crispy fried small fish served as a traditional Alfama bar snack, best enjoyed with a glass of white wine at a riverside tavern.
Shredded salt cod fried with matchstick potatoes and topped with black olives and fried onions, a beloved Portuguese classic found throughout the district.
Fresh grilled sardines simply seasoned with sea salt and lemon, seasonal delicacy that defines Alfama's rustic coastal dining culture.
Enjoy warm custard tarts at a traditional café while listening to street fado musicians, capturing the neighborhood's soul through food and music.

culture.detail: Respect the emotional intensity of fado performances by remaining silent and attentive; this melancholic music form is considered a spiritual expression of Portuguese identity.
The medieval layout was never planned with numbered streets or clear directions, so embrace getting lost and ask locals for directions rather than relying solely on maps.
Avoid overly touristy restaurants with laminated menus and instead seek family-run tascas (small taverns) where residents dine and fado is genuinely performed.
Alfama comes alive after sunset when fado venues fill with locals and visitors; daytime can feel quiet, so plan major activities and dining for evening hours.

places.name: Óbidos
places.description: A picturesque fortified medieval village north of Lisbon enclosed by complete castle walls, with whitewashed houses and cobblestone streets. Famous as a literary town and for its romantic ambiance, it's one of Portugal's most photogenic destinations.

activities.detail: Circumnavigate the complete medieval fortifications that encircle Óbidos for panoramic views of the village and surrounding countryside.
Explore the 12th-century castle keep which now houses a hotel but allows day visitors to tour its tower and ramparts for historical perspective.
Discover independent bookshops and literary-themed cafés scattered throughout the village that celebrate Óbidos' status as Portugal's first UNESCO City of Literature.
Wander the narrow cobblestone alleys lined with characteristic whitewashed houses adorned with blue and yellow trim, stopping at artisan shops and galleries.
Tour this modest museum housed in a former mansion displaying religious art, ceramics, and local historical artifacts from various periods.
If visiting in April, participate in the annual Óbidos International Chocolate Festival featuring artisan chocolatiers and chocolate-themed events.

food.detail: Sample the local cherry liqueur served in a small edible chocolate cup at one of the village's traditional tascas or gift shops.
Enjoy this traditional Portuguese fish stew made with mixed fresh catches and vegetables, typical of the coastal region north of Lisbon.
Taste these custard-filled pastries with crispy, flaky pastry layers at local bakeries, a quintessential Portuguese sweet treat.
Savor seafood rice cooked with local shellfish, shrimp, and broth that reflects the proximity to both the ocean and Óbidos Lagoon.
Try these traditional small cheese pastries made with local whey cheese and cinnamon, a regional specialty you'll find in most bakeries.

culture.text: Visit Early Morning or Late Afternoon
culture.detail: Arrive before 10 a.m. or after 4 p.m. to avoid peak tourist crowds and experience the village's authentic atmosphere and better photography light.
Wear sturdy walking shoes as the entire village is built on uneven medieval cobblestone streets with steep inclines, especially near the walls.
Remember that local residents still inhabit the medieval houses; be mindful when photographing and keep noise levels low, particularly in residential areas.
Understand that the village was gifted by King Denis to his bride Isabella in 1282, establishing its romantic legacy that later inspired its literary designation.

places.name: Cabo da Roca
places.description: The westernmost point of continental Europe, marked by a dramatic cliff lighthouse overlooking the Atlantic Ocean near Sintra. A must-visit for its spectacular coastal views and the romantic significance of standing at Europe's edge.

activities.detail: Explore the iconic red and white striped lighthouse perched on the cliff edge, which has guided ships since 1772 and offers panoramic Atlantic views.
Follow the dramatic coastal trails that hug the 140-meter cliffs with breathtaking views of the Atlantic Ocean and the rocky coastline below.

activities.text: Get the Cabo da Roca Certificate
activities.detail: Purchase an official certificate at the visitor center confirming you have visited Europe's westernmost continental point, a popular souvenir for travelers.
Witness the dramatic sunset over the Atlantic from the cliff edge, one of Portugal's most photographed natural phenomena.
Visit the nearby hilltop town of Sintra, just 30 kilometers away, famous for its romantic palaces including Pena Palace and Quinta da Regaleira.

food.detail: Fresh Atlantic sardines grilled over charcoal, a traditional Portuguese coastal preparation found at local restaurants near Cabo da Roca.
A traditional copper pan seafood stew combining shellfish, fish, and aromatics, reflecting the region's rich maritime heritage.
Shredded salt cod mixed with thin-cut fried potatoes and black olives, a beloved Portuguese dish available throughout the Sintra region.
Creamy custard tarts with crispy pastry layers and cinnamon, a specialty of nearby Sintra that pairs perfectly with Portuguese coffee.
Local Atlantic white fish prepared simply grilled or baked with traditional Portuguese hot peppers and garlic.

culture.detail: The cliff winds are powerful and unpredictable; stay behind safety barriers and be cautious with loose items, as the Atlantic coast can turn quickly.

culture.text: Visit Early or Late to Avoid Crowds
culture.detail: Cabo da Roca is extremely popular with tour groups; visiting at dawn or sunset offers a more peaceful experience and better photography opportunities.
Locals appreciate polite greetings in Portuguese; a simple 'Olá' or 'Boa tarde' goes a long way, and tipping 5-10% is customary at restaurants.
The site holds deep romantic symbolism in Portuguese culture as the 'end of the land and the beginning of the sea,' inspiring poets and explorers throughout history.

places.name: Bairro Alto District
places.description: Lisbon's trendy hillside neighborhood famous for its vibrant nightlife, street art, vintage shops, and traditional tascas serving authentic Portuguese food. Ride the historic Elevador da Bica funicular and explore bohemian culture and contemporary creativity.
imageQueries: Bairro Alto Lisbon street art graffiti

activities.detail: Ride this historic wooden funicular railway from Calçada do Combro up the steep hillside, offering views over the Tagus and a quintessential Lisbon experience since 1884.
Explore the narrow streets and alleyways filled with vibrant murals, stencil work, and contemporary graffiti by local and international artists that define Bairro Alto's bohemian character.

activities.text: Vintage Shopping on Rua da Atalaia
activities.detail: Browse independent vintage boutiques, second-hand record shops, and quirky antique stores selling everything from retro clothing to Portuguese ceramics and vinyl records.
Visit this 16th-century Jesuit church with its ornate interior and museum containing precious religious artifacts and Portuguese azulejo tiles.

activities.text: Miradouro de São Pedro de Alcântara
activities.detail: Climb to this hilltop viewpoint near the church to enjoy panoramic views of Lisbon's downtown, the castle, and the Tagus River, perfect for sunset watching.
Experience Bairro Alto's legendary nightlife by moving between intimate tascas, bohemian bars, and late-night clubs that come alive after midnight with locals and visitors.

food.detail: A pressed sandwich layered with cured meats, cheese, and a rich beer sauce that's hearty and indulgent, often served in small tasca restaurants throughout the neighborhood.
A creamy, flavorful rice dish loaded with fresh seafood including clams, mussels, and shrimp, representing authentic Portuguese coastal cuisine adapted for Lisbon.
Crispy custard tarts with caramelized edges and creamy filling that are best enjoyed warm from local bakeries, a quintessential Portuguese pastry found everywhere in the district.
A traditional Portuguese smoked sausage made with bread, meat, and garlic, typically grilled and served as a tasca appetizer or light meal with mustard.
Eat at one of the traditional hole-in-the-wall taverns serving simple, authentic Portuguese home cooking with wine in intimate, convivial settings full of locals and character.

culture.detail: Bairro Alto truly comes alive after 11 PM when locals gather in bars and restaurants; visiting earlier in the evening means missing the authentic neighborhood energy and community spirit.
Many residents live above bars and restaurants; while nightlife is celebrated, keeping noise respectful in early morning hours and being aware of neighbors reflects local courtesy.
Traditional tascas and many vintage shops operate on a cash-only basis, so carrying euros ensures you can fully experience the neighborhood's authentic local establishments without difficulty.
Bairro Alto's hilly terrain with narrow, uneven cobblestone streets requires comfortable walking shoes and careful footing, especially at night when streets are crowded and dimly lit.

places.name: Palmela
places.description: A historic town south of Lisbon dominated by a medieval castle overlooking wine country and the Tagus valley. Known for wine production and its annual music festival, it offers cultural heritage and scenic rural countryside views.

activities.detail: Explore this 12th-century hilltop fortress with panoramic views over the Tagus valley and surrounding wine estates, featuring restored ramparts and a small museum.
Visit local wineries and quintas producing renowned Palmela wines, particularly red blends and Moscatel, with tastings and cellar tours available.
View this 16th-century parish church in the town center featuring Manueline architecture and traditional Portuguese azulejo tilework.

activities.text: Festa da Música (Festa NOS Primavera Sound equivalent)
activities.detail: Attend the major annual music festival typically held in summer, drawing international and Portuguese artists to outdoor venues across the town.
Hike through rural trails surrounding Palmela past cork oak groves and small agricultural holdings offering authentic rural landscape views.
Visit the Carmelite convent ruins nearby Palmela, a serene historical site surrounded by natural countryside.

food.detail: Taste the region's famous sweet fortified wine made from Muscat grapes, a traditional dessert wine unique to Palmela's terroir.
Sample this seafood rice dish combining local ingredients with traditional Portuguese coastal cooking techniques.
Enjoy shredded fish fried in thin julienne and served with potato straw and olives, a classic Setúbal region preparation.
Try homemade farmstead cheese from local producers, often paired with wine at rural properties and local markets.
Taste these regional pastries traditionally filled with chocolate or custard, sold at local bakeries and festival stalls.

culture.detail: Visit during September-October to experience vendimia celebrations, but respect private quinta properties and only enter wineries with prior arrangements or official tours.
Many local shops and restaurants close from 13:00 to 15:00 for lunch, and the town adopts a slow afternoon pace reflecting rural Portuguese customs.
Locals and visitors traditionally gather at Palmela Castle for sunset viewpoints and seasonal celebrations, making it a social hub rather than just a tourist attraction.
Learning basic wine terminology and showing genuine interest in local winemakers' stories earns respect and often leads to more authentic cellar experiences beyond standard tastings.

places.name: Queluz
places.description: Home to the stunning Queluz National Palace, an 18th-century rococo residence that served as a royal summer retreat. Now a museum, it showcases exquisite architecture, formal gardens, and Portuguese royal history just outside Lisbon.

activities.detail: Explore the magnificent 18th-century rococo palace with its ornate rooms, throne chamber, and royal apartments that reveal Portuguese royal history and aristocratic life.
Stroll through the meticulously manicured baroque and rococo gardens featuring geometric patterns, fountains, topiary, and landscaped terraces surrounding the palace.
Observe the intricate hand-painted Portuguese ceramic tiles decorating palace walls, corridors, and gardens that exemplify traditional azulejo craftsmanship.
View royal furnishings, paintings, sculptures, and decorative arts spanning centuries of Portuguese and European aristocratic taste and craftsmanship.
Walk through the charming streets surrounding the palace to discover traditional Portuguese architecture, local shops, and neighborhood cafés away from Lisbon crowds.

food.detail: Crispy, flaky pastry tarts with creamy custard filling and cinnamon are a quintessential Portuguese treat available in local bakeries near Queluz.
A hearty Portuguese sandwich with roasted meats, cheese, and a savory beer sauce that reflects the country's casual dining traditions.
A rich seafood rice dish combining shrimp, mussels, and other shellfish cooked with saffron and broth, representing Portuguese coastal cuisine.
A traditional green vegetable soup made with kale, potatoes, and olive oil that is both comforting and authentically Portuguese in every local establishment.
Shredded salted cod mixed with crispy fried potatoes and olives, a beloved Portuguese classic available in regional restaurants and family-run eateries.

culture.detail: Plan your visit carefully as the palace maintains specific operating hours and occasionally closes for royal events or maintenance; check ahead before traveling.
Many local shops and cafés may close between 1–3 PM for a midday break, so schedule your exploration accordingly to avoid disappointment when seeking refreshments.
While photography is generally permitted in palace gardens and exteriors, some interior rooms may restrict flash or tripods, so check signage and respect posted guidelines.
Locals appreciate visitors who greet with a simple 'Olá' or 'Bom dia' and make efforts to speak basic Portuguese; small courtesies enhance your welcome in this cultural space.

places.name: Tagus Estuary Nature Reserve
places.nameIt: Reserva Natural do Estuário do Tejo
places.description: A vital wetland habitat east of Lisbon protecting thousands of migrating birds and diverse ecosystems along the Tagus River. Ideal for birdwatching, nature photography, and understanding Portugal's natural conservation efforts.

activities.detail: Observe thousands of migratory waders, herons, and spoonbills in their natural habitat across the salt ponds and mudflats.
Paddle through narrow waterways bordered by reed beds and marshlands to spot otters, fish eagles, and wading birds up close.

activities.text: Visit the Interpretive Centre at Pancas
activities.detail: Learn about the estuary's ecosystems, conservation efforts, and migratory patterns through exhibits and guided nature walks.
Capture stunning images of roosting flamingos, avocets, and sandpipers against the backdrop of the estuary at sunrise or sunset.
Walk marked paths through cork oak forests and wetland edges that offer panoramic views of the estuary and surrounding agricultural landscapes.

activities.text: Tidal Flat Exploration at Lezíria Grande
activities.detail: Wade through shallow tidal areas during low tide to observe crabs, mollusks, and shorebirds in their feeding grounds.

food.detail: A creamy rice dish cooked with local shellfish from the estuary including clams, mussels, and shrimp, reflecting the region's maritime heritage.

food.text: Eel Stew (Enguias à Moda de Aveiro)
food.detail: Fresh eels from the estuary braised with tomatoes, garlic, and coriander in a traditional preparation from Portugal's central coast.
Whole sea bass caught from local waters, grilled simply with olive oil, lemon, and sea salt at riverside restaurants.
Tender crustaceans harvested from rocky outcrops along the estuary mouth, boiled and served with garlic butter as a delicacy.
Local artisanal bread paired with creamy white cheese from inland farms, reflecting the agricultural tradition surrounding the reserve.

culture.detail: Many bird species breed between March and July; stay on designated paths and maintain distance to avoid disturbing nests and reducing breeding success.
The estuary supports traditional fishing communities who warmly share knowledge about seasonal bird arrivals and sustainable harvesting practices if approached respectfully.

The reserve is a source of pride for local environmental efforts; visitors will find guides and locals eager to explain Portugal's commitment to protecting this Ramsar wetland of international importance.

restaurants.name: Belcanto
restaurants.description: Two Michelin stars showcasing innovative Portuguese cuisine with exceptional technical skill and local ingredients.
restaurants.mustOrder: Chef's tasting menu with seasonal vegetables and Portuguese seafood

restaurants.name: Pharmaceutica Portuguesa
restaurants.address: Rua das Portas de Santo Antão 2, Lisboa
restaurants.description: Historic converted pharmacy restaurant serving traditional Portuguese fare in an atmospheric vintage setting.

restaurants.name: Alma
restaurants.description: Intimate one Michelin star restaurant in Alfama celebrating seasonal Portuguese ingredients with refined technique.

restaurants.name: Casa de Comidas
restaurants.description: Charming family-run tavern serving honest, traditional Portuguese home cooking in a cozy neighborhood setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Bacalhau à Brás (salt cod with matchstick potatoes)

restaurants.name: A Travessa
restaurants.address: Travessa do Convento das Bernardas 2A, Lisboa
restaurants.description: Converted warehouse restaurant with creative international cuisine, art installations, and sophisticated atmosphere.

restaurants.name: O Fado
restaurants.description: Authentic fado restaurant in Alfama serving traditional Portuguese dishes with live music performances.

restaurants.name: Gambrinus
restaurants.address: Rua das Portas de Santo Antão 25, Lisboa
restaurants.description: Legendary seafood restaurant since 1936 with wood-paneled interiors and exceptional fresh fish and shellfish.

restaurants.name: Taverna do Embuçado
restaurants.description: Budget-friendly hole-in-the-wall in Alfama serving generous portions of traditional Portuguese home cooking.

restaurants.name: Minibar
restaurants.address: Rua Portas de Santo Antão 58, Lisboa
restaurants.description: Chef José Avillez's sophisticated minibar concept with creative small plates and impeccable execution.
restaurants.mustOrder: Codfish creamed soup in a cup

restaurants.name: Cantinho do Avillez
restaurants.description: Chef José Avillez's casual restaurant serving elevated Portuguese classics in a lively downtown setting.

restaurants.name: O Asiático
restaurants.description: Casual restaurant blending Portuguese tradition with Asian influences in a vibrant riverside neighborhood.
restaurants.mustOrder: Curry spiced octopus or fusion rice bowls

restaurants.name: Solar dos Presuntos
restaurants.address: Rua das Portas de Santo Antão 150, Lisboa
restaurants.description: Traditional Portuguese restaurant established in 1974 serving excellent cured meats, seafood, and regional specialties.
restaurants.mustOrder: Presunto ibérico (Iberian ham) and roasted fish

restaurants.name: Rei dos Frangos
restaurants.description: Famous no-frills grilled chicken restaurant in Caparica district, beloved by locals for quality and value.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled chicken with piri piri and rice

restaurants.name: Vela Latina
restaurants.description: Beachfront seafood restaurant in Cascais with fresh daily catches and panoramic Atlantic views.

restaurants.name: O Bouquet da Sintra
restaurants.description: Traditional restaurant in historic Sintra serving authentic Portuguese regional cuisine with local character.

regions.name: Minho and Douro
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from September to October for harvest season and perfect weather in Portugal's premier wine region.

regions.name: Beira Interior
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Best visited in spring or autumn to enjoy the mountainous landscape and avoid harsh winters.

regions.name: Beira Litoral
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from May to September for mild weather and to explore both coastal and mountain areas.

regions.name: Estremadura
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from June to September for warm weather and beach activities along this picturesque coastline.

regions.name: Ribatejo
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Best visited in spring or autumn to experience horse culture and riverside landscapes comfortably.

regions.name: Central Alentejo
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from October to April to avoid intense summer heat while exploring Roman ruins and medieval towns.

regions.name: Lower Alentejo
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Best visited from October to April when temperatures are moderate and wildflowers bloom.

regions.name: Algarve
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from April to October for sunshine and beach weather, though summer crowds are intense.

regions.name: Madeira
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit year-round for mild climate, but April to June offers the best combination of weather and fewer crowds.

regions.name: Azores
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from June to September for the warmest and most stable weather in these remote Atlantic islands.

regions.name: Lisbon Region
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from April to June or September to October for pleasant weather and manageable tourist crowds.

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 41 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: Portugal's primary national public holiday marking the start of the new year with celebrations across the country.

festivals.name: Festa da Rainha Santa
festivals.description: Religious festival honoring Queen Isabel in Covilhã, featuring processions and traditional celebrations of this local holy day.

festivals.name: Carnival
festivals.description: Portugal's most vibrant carnival celebration with parades, street parties, and traditional festivities across all regions, most famous in Madeira and Loulé.

festivals.name: Madeira Carnival
festivals.description: One of Europe's largest carnivals featuring elaborate floats, samba dancers, and vibrant street parades lasting several days.

festivals.name: Fantasporto
festivals.description: International film festival in Porto showcasing fantasy, horror, and science fiction cinema with prestigious awards and premieres.

festivals.name: Loulé Carnival
festivals.description: The Algarve's most famous carnival with satirical floats, street parades, and traditional costume competitions reflecting regional humor and creativity.

festivals.name: Festa da Flor
festivals.description: Colorful flower festival celebrating Madeira's blooming season with street decorations, parades, and exhibitions of local flora.

festivals.name: Easter
festivals.description: Portugal's major religious holiday with processions, religious services, and traditional family celebrations observed nationwide.

festivals.name: Festa de São Jorge
festivals.description: Azorean celebration of St. George with traditional festivities, street decorations, and local food traditions across the islands.

festivals.name: Festa dos Rapazes
festivals.description: Ancient Easter tradition in the mountains where young men dress in colorful costumes and perform satirical skits and dances.
festivals.location: Sortelha and other Beira Interior villages

festivals.name: Day of Portugal
festivals.description: Portugal's national day celebrating Portuguese culture, history, and identity with official ceremonies and cultural events nationwide.

festivals.name: Festa de Santo António
festivals.description: Lisbon's most beloved street festival honoring St. Anthony with popular saints' day celebrations, street parties, and traditional grilled sardines.

festivals.name: Festa de São João
festivals.description: Traditional midsummer festival in Porto and across northern Portugal with bonfires, street parties, and traditional food and music.

festivals.name: Festa de São Pedro
festivals.description: Saint Peter's Day celebrations particularly vibrant in fishing communities with blessing of boats, parades, and traditional festivities.

festivals.name: NOS Primavera Sound
festivals.description: International music festival in Porto attracting major contemporary artists across multiple genres with three days of performances.

festivals.name: Festa da Rainha Santa
festivals.description: Five-yearly procession in Covilhã (next in 2024) honoring Queen Isabel with elaborate ceremonies and ancient traditions.

festivals.name: Festa de Nossa Senhora da Guia
festivals.description: Religious pilgrimage festival in Cascais honoring Our Lady of the Guide with processions and traditional religious ceremonies.

festivals.name: Festival NOS Alive
festivals.description: Major international music festival near Lisbon in Caparica featuring diverse artists across rock, pop, and electronic genres.

festivals.name: Festa de São Cristóvão
festivals.description: Religious festival in Machico honoring St. Christopher with traditional island celebrations and cultural events.

festivals.name: Festa da Ria
festivals.description: Festival in Aveiro celebrating the coastal lagoon with cultural performances, traditional food, and boat parades showcasing regional heritage.

festivals.name: Festa de Santa Maria
festivals.description: Assumption of Mary celebrations across the Azores with religious processions and traditional island festivities.

festivals.name: Festa de São Lourenço
festivals.description: Traditional festival in Santarém featuring bullfighting, horse parades, and regional food celebrating St. Lawrence and Ribatejo culture.

festivals.name: Festa de Nossa Senhora do Cabo
festivals.description: Religious pilgrimage festival in Cabo de São Vicente honoring Our Lady with traditional ceremonies and religious observances.

festivals.name: Festival de Músicas do Mundo
festivals.description: World music festival in Sortelha celebrating diverse musical traditions from around the globe in a historic medieval village setting.

festivals.name: Festa de Nossa Senhora da Rocha
festivals.description: Beach festival in Armação da Pêra celebrating Our Lady with traditional religious ceremonies and village festivities.

festivals.name: Festa da Vindima
festivals.description: Harvest festival in Estremoz celebrating wine production with parades, grape treading, and traditional Alentejo cuisine.

festivals.name: Festival de Gastronomia
festivals.description: Culinary festival celebrating Algarve's diverse food traditions with cooking demonstrations, tastings, and regional specialties.

festivals.name: Festa da Castanha
festivals.description: Chestnut festival in mountain villages celebrating autumn harvest with roasted chestnuts, crafts, and traditional mountain culture.

festivals.name: Republic Day
festivals.description: National holiday commemorating the establishment of Portugal's Republic in 1910 with official ceremonies and celebrations.

festivals.name: Festa de São Lucas
festivals.description: Traditional festival in Borba honoring St. Luke with horse parades, regional food, and local wine celebrations.

festivals.name: Festa de Nossa Senhora da Conceição
festivals.description: Immaculate Conception celebrations in Madeira with religious processions and traditional island festivities.

festivals.name: Festa de Todos os Santos
festivals.description: All Saints' Day public holiday with families visiting cemeteries and traditional religious observances across Portugal.

festivals.name: Festa de São Martinho
festivals.description: St. Martin's Day festival with roasted chestnuts, new wine tastings, and traditional bonfires celebrating autumn.

festivals.name: Festa de São Brás de Alportel
festivals.description: Traditional festival in São Brás de Alportel honoring St. Blaise with folk celebrations, traditional costumes, and regional food.

festivals.name: Festa de São Vicente
festivals.description: Patron saint festival in Lagos honoring St. Vincent with religious ceremonies and local celebrations.

festivals.name: Festa de São Brás
festivals.description: Traditional festival celebrating St. Blaise with folk traditions, religious services, and regional cultural performances.

festivals.name: Festa de São Gonçalo
festivals.description: Winter festival honoring St. Gonçalo in Amarante with traditional river celebrations and regional festivities.

festivals.name: Christmas
festivals.description: Portugal's major religious and public holiday celebrating the birth of Christ with family gatherings, church services, and traditional festivities.

festivals.name: Festa do Divino Espírito Santo
festivals.description: Ancient Azorean celebration honoring the Holy Spirit with 50 days of festivities, processions, and traditional island ceremonies.

festivals.name: Festa da Cerveja
festivals.description: Beer festival celebrating brewing traditions with tastings, food pairings, and entertainment in a festive setting.

festivals.name: Festival de Chocolate
festivals.description: Chocolate festival in Óbidos celebrating Portuguese and international chocolate with tastings, workshops, and artisan products.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Portugal
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 15 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Prehistoric Portugal
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers and early farmers shaped the Iberian peninsula's first settlements.
items.text: After the last ice age retreated, nomadic peoples followed game across what is now Portugal, gradually settling into more permanent communities. By 5000 BC, agriculture arrived from the east, and the Neolithic peoples built megalithic tombs and stone circles that still dot the landscape. The Bronze Age brought metalworking and trade networks connecting Portugal to Mediterranean civilizations. These early inhabitants developed a culture distinctly different from their Spanish neighbors, establishing patterns of settlement and survival that would persist for millennia.

items.label: Iron Age & Lusitanians
items.summary: Indigenous Lusitanian peoples built hillforts and resisted outsiders with fierce independence.
items.text: The Lusitanians emerged as the dominant ethnic group in what is now Portugal, establishing a warrior culture centered around fortified settlements and pastoral life. They were known for their skilled horsemanship and ambush tactics, which would later make them formidable opponents to Rome. Trade with Phoenicians and Greeks brought Mediterranean influences, but the Lusitanians largely maintained their own language and customs. Their decentralized society, organized around powerful families and chieftains, made them difficult to conquer but also prevented unified resistance to foreign invasion.

items.label: Roman Conquest
items.summary: Rome's gradual conquest of Iberia took nearly two centuries, with Lusitania resisting fiercely.
items.text: When Rome invaded Iberia during the Second Punic War, the Lusitanians found themselves facing an empire determined to expand. For over a century, they fought a guerrilla war against Roman legions, with legendary leaders like Viriathus becoming symbols of resistance. The Romans struggled in the rugged terrain and eventually resorted to assassination and treachery to defeat their opponent. By 19 BC, Augustus finally pacified the last rebellious regions, and Portugal entered the Roman fold as the province of Lusitania, bringing roads, cities, Latin language, and Christianity.

items.label: Roman Lusitania
items.summary: Roman rule transformed Portugal with urban centers, agriculture, and the Latin language that became Portuguese.
items.text: Under Roman administration, Portugal flourished economically as olive oil, wine, and grain flowed to markets across the empire. Cities like Covilhã and Braga grew into prosperous centers of commerce and culture, while aqueducts and roads connected settlements. The Latin language gradually replaced Lusitanian, eventually evolving into the Romance language that would become Portuguese. In the later empire, Christianity spread through the region, replacing pagan traditions and setting the foundation for Portugal's future religious identity.

items.label: Visigothic Rule
items.summary: Germanic Visigoths claimed the peninsula and imposed their own order after Rome's collapse.
items.text: As Roman power crumbled, Visigothic tribes from Central Europe migrated into Iberia and established a kingdom that would last for three centuries. Portugal became part of this wider Visigothic realm, though local authorities and bishops often wielded considerable power. The Visigoths adopted much of Roman civilization, maintaining the church, tax systems, and cities while introducing Germanic legal codes. By the 7th century, Visigothic rule had become fragmented, with rival factions competing for the throne and setting the stage for the arrival of North African Muslims.

items.label: Muslim Conquest & Al-Andalus
items.summary: North African Berber and Arab forces conquered the peninsula and ruled Portugal for half a millennium.
items.text: In 711, Muslim armies from North Africa crossed the Strait of Gibraltar, swiftly overwhelmed Visigothic forces, and established Al-Andalus, an Islamic state that would transform Iberia. Portuguese territories became the southern frontier of this expanding Islamic civilization, where Christians, Jews, and Muslims coexisted in an intricate social hierarchy. Moorish rulers brought advanced agriculture, mathematics, astronomy, and architecture, creating cities of remarkable sophistication. However, Christian kingdoms in the north gradually pushed back, and by the mid-13th century, the last Muslim strongholds in Portugal had fallen to Christian forces.

items.label: Portuguese Independence
items.summary: A small Christian county broke free from Spanish overlordship and established Portugal as a sovereign kingdom.
items.text: Afonso Henriques, Count of Portucale, defeated Muslim forces at the Battle of Ourique in 1139 and declared himself king of an independent Portugal, though it took until 1297 for Spanish kingdoms to fully recognize his new realm. The early Portuguese kings focused on expanding southward at Muslim expense, gradually conquering the Algarve and pushing back the frontier of Islamic rule. This period forged a Portuguese national identity distinct from Castile and Aragon, shaped by military conquest and religious crusading. By the end of the 13th century, Portugal had essentially reached its modern borders, making it one of Europe's oldest nation-states.

items.label: Medieval Kingdom
items.summary: A consolidated Portuguese kingdom developed commerce, culture, and a powerful navy that would soon explore the world.
items.text: After securing its borders, Portugal focused on building a strong central government, establishing trade networks, and cultivating military power. Kings like Dinis promoted agriculture, education, and the arts, creating a flourishing medieval culture centered in Lisbon and Porto. The Portuguese crown granted charters to cities and merchants, encouraging economic growth and the rise of a merchant class that would finance overseas expansion. The late 14th and 15th centuries saw Portugal grow wealthy from trade with England, the Mediterranean, and Africa, while Portuguese shipbuilders pioneered new vessel designs that would enable global exploration.

items.label: Age of Discovery
items.summary: Portuguese explorers and traders built a global maritime empire that enriched the kingdom and changed the world.
items.text: Prince Henry the Navigator's sponsored expeditions gradually pushed Portuguese ships down the African coast, eventually reaching India, the Americas, and Asia. Vasco da Gama rounded the Cape of Good Hope in 1497, opening a sea route to India and launching Portugal into global dominance as a trading power. Portuguese merchants and sailors established coastal forts and trading posts across Africa, the Indian Ocean, and the Atlantic, creating a sprawling commercial empire. Gold from Africa and spices from Asia flooded into Lisbon, enriching the crown and making Portugal one of Europe's wealthiest and most influential nations during the 16th century.

items.label: Spanish Union & Decline
items.summary: Portugal lost independence to Spain for sixty years, and its global empire began to crumble.
items.text: When the last Portuguese king died without an heir, the Spanish King Philip II claimed the throne, uniting the two crowns and ending Portugal's independence. Dutch and English raiders took advantage of the distraction to attack Portuguese trading posts across the globe, gradually dismantling the maritime empire. The Portuguese people, however, never forgot their separate identity, and resistance to Spanish rule grew throughout the 60-year union. The strain of Spanish wars, religious upheaval, and economic decline weakened Portugal's position in global trade and empowered a restless nobility and merchant class.

items.label: Portuguese Restoration
items.summary: A successful revolution restored Portuguese independence and rebuilt the kingdom under dynamic new leadership.
items.text: On December 1, 1640, Portuguese nobles and commoners rose up and overthrew Spanish rule, placing the Duke of Bragança on the throne as King João IV. The restoration sparked nearly 30 years of warfare with Spain, but Portugal's determination and military skill secured international recognition of independence. King João V, enriched by gold discoveries in Brazil, launched ambitious building programs and cultural projects that restored Lisbon to grandeur. However, this period of rebuilding came to a catastrophic end in 1755 when a massive earthquake destroyed much of Lisbon and exposed the fragility of Portugal's new stability.

items.label: Pombal & Enlightenment Reform
items.summary: An authoritarian minister rebuilt Portugal with enlightened reforms, expelling the Jesuits and modernizing the state.
items.text: The earthquake's devastation gave Prime Minister Marquês de Pombal an opportunity to impose sweeping reforms on Portuguese society, rebuilding Lisbon with rational urban planning and modern infrastructure. Pombal clashed with the Catholic Church, expelling the Jesuits and asserting state supremacy over religious institutions in ways that foreshadowed 19th-century conflicts. He reformed education, commerce, and administration along rationalist lines, aiming to transform Portugal into a modern European state. His authoritarian methods created both progress and resentment, leaving a complicated legacy that influenced Portuguese politics for generations.

items.label: Liberal Wars & Republican Struggle
items.summary: Portugal fractured between reactionary monarchy and liberal reformers in a century of political upheaval and instability.
items.text: A liberal revolution in 1820 attempted to impose constitutional limits on royal power, sparking a civil war that lasted until 1834 and weakened the monarchy's authority. The 19th century saw Portugal buffeted by competing factions: traditionalists defending the church and aristocracy, liberals pushing for constitutional reform, and growing republican sentiment among intellectuals and urban workers. Portuguese politics became notorious for instability, with dozens of governments rising and falling while the nation lost territory in Africa and fell into economic decline. A revolution in 1910 finally abolished the monarchy and established a republic, but political chaos continued as successive governments struggled to modernize a deeply divided society.

items.label: Republic & Dictatorship
items.summary: A weak republic gave way to a military dictatorship that ruled Portugal for nearly half a century.
items.text: The First Portuguese Republic promised modernization but delivered chaos, with 16 governments in 16 years and widespread social conflict. In 1926, a military coup overthrew the republic, installing a conservative dictatorship under António de Oliveira Salazar that would govern until 1968. Salazar imposed authoritarian control, censorship, and brutal secret police surveillance while maintaining Portugal's African colonies long after other European powers had decolonized. His successor Marcelo Caetano tried gradual reform but repressed colonial independence movements with military force, generating a costly war that drained the nation and discredited the regime.

items.label: Carnation Revolution & Democracy
items.summary: A peaceful military coup restored democracy, decolonized Africa, and integrated Portugal into modern Europe.
items.text: On April 25, 1974, junior military officers overthrew the dictatorship in a bloodless revolution remembered for soldiers distributing carnations to jubilant crowds. The new democratic government granted independence to Portuguese African colonies, ending 500 years of imperial rule and redefining Portugal's global role. Portugal joined NATO in 1949 and the European Union in 1986, gradually integrating into Western institutions and modernizing its economy, education, and social systems. Today, Portugal is a stable democracy and prosperous EU member, having transformed from a dictatorship and colonial power into a respected European nation, though it continues balancing traditional Mediterranean culture with contemporary European values.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Portugal — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Order at the counter, not your table
items.text: In Portugal, you'll pay less if you stand at the counter (balcão) than sitting at a table. A café (espresso) at the counter costs €0.60–1.00; the same coffee seated costs €1.50–2.50. This is standard practice, not rude.

items.label: Ask for a 'meia de leite' for milky coffee
items.text: If you want coffee with warm milk, order a meia de leite (literally 'half milk'). A café com leite is stronger. Don't expect a large cappuccino; Portuguese coffee culture favors small, strong shots.

items.label: Pastéis de Nata go with morning coffee
items.text: The creamy custard tart is the quintessential Portuguese pastry. Buy them fresh from pastelarias (bakeries) in the morning—they're best within hours of baking. The famous ones in Belém, Lisbon are good but touristy and pricey.

items.label: Afternoon coffee means something sweet
items.text: Portuguese people take a bica (espresso) with a small pastry mid-morning or after lunch. Cafés are social spaces; lingering over one coffee is normal and welcomed.

items.label: Bacalhau à Brás
items.text: Shredded salt cod, fried into crispy strands, mixed with thin-cut potatoes and black olives. It's one of Portugal's most iconic dishes—creamy, salty, and addictive. Found everywhere from casual tascas to fine dining.

items.label: Pastéis de Tentúgal
items.text: A specialty from the Tentúgal region: crispy pastry tubes filled with sweet egg cream. Lighter and less rich than Pastéis de Nata, they're harder to find but worth seeking out if you're passing through Covilhã or Aveiro.

items.label: Arroz de Marisco
items.text: Seafood risotto loaded with shrimp, clams, and sometimes crab, cooked in a savory broth. A coastal specialty best eaten in fishing towns like Nazaré or Setúbal, though Lisbon restaurants do it well.

items.label: Francesinha
items.text: A Porto sandwich of layered meats, cheese, and a rich beer sauce. It's heavy, indulgent, and unmissably Portuguese—though eating one per meal as a tourist will exhaust you. One per trip is plenty.

items.label: Caldo Verde
items.text: A humble soup of potato, kale, and chorizo, finished with olive oil. It's comfort food in a bowl and appears on nearly every Portuguese menu. Order it at lunch or as a starter; it's cheap and warming.

items.label: Sardines Grelhadas
items.text: Whole grilled sardines, simply salted and dressed with lemon and olive oil. Peak season is May to October. If you see fresh sardines on a menu near the coast, order them—they're sweet and melt in your mouth.

items.label: Ovos Moles
items.text: A sweet treat from Aveiro: soft, creamy egg paste in a thin wafer shell, sometimes shaped like shells or fish. Intensely sugary; buy one or two to taste rather than a box.

items.label: Look for 'Prato do Dia' (Dish of the Day)
items.text: This is always affordable, fresh, and usually a local specialty. It's written on a board or mentioned verbally—you may need to ask. Expect to pay €8–12 for a full meal.

items.label: Bread and olives aren't free
items.text: If the waiter brings bread, olives, or butter to your table, you'll be charged for them (usually €1–3 per item). Politely decline if you don't want them. They're not complimentary like in Spain.

items.label: Ask about 'Especialidades da Casa'
items.text: Restaurants take pride in house specials. The waiter will often recommend their best or most unique dish—this is genuine pride, not a upsell. These are worth ordering.

items.label: Understand portion sizes
items.text: Meia dose (half portion) is widely available and often enough for one person. A full prato (plate) is generous. Two people can comfortably share three half portions and sides.

items.label: Menus in English may mean tourist trap
items.text: Waterfront restaurants and areas with English-only menus often charge 30–50% more and serve mediocre food. Seek out places with Portuguese menus, locals eating, and waiters who speak little English—that's where quality is.

items.label: Tasca or Tascaria (working-class eatery)
items.text: These hole-in-the-wall spots serve simple, cheap, authentic food. No frills, often no menu—the owner will tell you what's being cooked today. Lunch is €6–9; this is where locals eat. Perfect for experiencing real Portuguese food.

items.label: Pastelaria (bakery/café)
items.text: For breakfast, pastries, and coffee. These are ubiquitous, affordable, and a genuine way to start your day. Stand at the counter, grab a pastry and bica, and watch Portuguese life unfold.

items.label: Marisqueira (seafood restaurant)
items.text: Specializing in grilled fish and shellfish, these are essential near the coast. Quality varies wildly; ask locals or look for full tables of Portuguese families at lunch. Beware waterfront tourist traps.

items.label: Adega (wine bar/casual restaurant)
items.text: A traditional space serving wine, cheese, cured meats, and simple cooked dishes. These are atmospheric and social—perfect for dinner and wine. Found throughout the country, especially in rural areas and wine regions.

items.label: Cervejaria (beer hall)
items.text: Large, casual spaces serving beer, seafood platters, and grilled meat. Lively and touristed in Lisbon and Porto, but genuine elsewhere. Good for groups and those wanting informal, loud energy.

items.label: Avoid restaurants with picture menus near major sights
items.text: Laminated menus with photos near tourist landmarks signal overpriced, mediocre food. Walk 2–3 blocks away from famous attractions to find honest local spots.

items.label: Order house wine by the glass or jug
items.text: Ask for vinho da casa (house wine). It's inexpensive (€2–4 per glass), often local, and paired with the chef's recommendations. A jug (jarro) serves 2–3 and costs €5–8. Skip the wine list unless you're splurging.

items.label: Vinho Verde (Young Green Wine)
items.text: A slightly fizzy, low-alcohol white wine from the north, best served ice-cold. It's refreshing, affordable, and perfect for seafood and warm weather. Despite the name, it's not always green—most are pale yellow.

items.label: Port Wine (Vinho do Porto)
items.text: A fortified wine from Porto, rich and complex. It's expensive in restaurants but worth a tasting glass after dinner. Visit a local adega or wine bar to learn the difference between Ruby (fruity, young) and Tawny (aged, complex).

items.label: Madeira Wine
items.text: Another fortified wine, from the Madeira islands. Warm, caramel-forward, and aged in casks. Less famous than Port but equally Portuguese. Try a glass in a traditional adega.

items.label: Beer is cheap and good
items.text: A small beer (mini, €1–2) or larger cerveja (€2–4) is everywhere and often better value than wine in casual spots. Super Bock and Sagres are the main brands; both are solid, crisp lagers.

items.label: Try a Ginjinha in Lisbon
items.text: A sweet, dark cherry liqueur from Lisbon's Óbidos region. It's traditionally drunk in a small glass, sometimes with a cherry at the bottom. Available in tiny bars called Ginjinhas—one shot is enough (€2–3).

items.label: Coffee-based digestifs after dinner
items.text: A shot of strong coffee with a splash of brandy or firewater (aguardente) is traditional. Ask for a bica com água ardente or simply a coffee digestivo. It's a local habit, not commonly ordered by tourists, but worth trying.

items.label: Dinner starts at 8 PM or later
items.text: Restaurants don't fill until 8:30–9 PM. Eating at 6 or 7 PM marks you as a tourist; arriving earlier may mean limited options or closed kitchens. If you're hungry early, eat at a café or snack bar.

items.label: Tipping is appreciated but not required
items.text: Leave 5–10% if service was good, or round up the bill. Service is included, and tipping won't make or break a restaurant. It's a gesture of satisfaction, not an obligation.

items.label: Greet staff with 'Olá' or 'Bom Dia/Boa Tarde'
items.text: A simple greeting when arriving and leaving shows respect. Portuguese hospitality is warm but formal—politeness opens doors. Say 'Obrigado/Obrigada' (thank you) and staff will be warmer.

items.label: Don't ask for modifications mid-service
items.text: Portuguese restaurants cook to a standard. Asking to remove an ingredient or change a dish after ordering is seen as insulting to the chef. Check the menu carefully before ordering; take-backs aren't common.

items.label: Lunch is the main meal (12:30–2 PM)
items.text: This is when Portuguese people eat their biggest meal, and restaurants offer the best deals. Dinner is lighter. If budget or time is tight, eat your main meal at lunch and a light bite at dinner.

items.label: Water isn't automatically free
items.text: Ask for água da torneira (tap water) if you want free water—it's safe and will be brought. Bottled water (água engarrafada) will be charged. Still or sparkling (com gás).

items.label: Smoking is common in older establishments
items.text: Legally banned indoors since 2008, but enforcement in older bars and tascas is lax. If smoke bothers you, eat at newer restaurants or tourist-friendly spots where rules are stricter.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Portugal.

items.label: Book with CP
items.text: Comboios de Portugal (CP) operates most long-distance trains. Book via cp.pt or the CP app for better prices, especially on Alfa Pendular (fast) and IC (intercity) services between Lisbon, Porto, Covilhã and the Algarve.

items.label: Regional trains are cheapest
items.text: Regiónal (R) and Comboio Regional Expresso (CRE) trains cost half the price of express services and work well for shorter hops, though they're slower and make many stops.
Strikes happen occasionally on CP lines, especially during industrial action. Check cp.pt for service updates before traveling, or use buses as backup.

items.label: Validate before boarding
items.text: Even with a ticket, validate it at the platform machine before entering—conductors may fine you €50+ if you can't show a stamped ticket.

items.label: Lisbon Metro is fastest
items.text: The Metropolitano de Lisboa is clean, safe and runs 6:30am–1am. Use the Viva Viagem card (€0.50) and load €6.40 for 10 journeys (€0.64 each), or buy a 7-day pass for €40.

items.label: Porto and regional buses
items.text: Porto has a light metro (STCP) but buses cover more ground; tap your Andante card (€0.60) or buy a 10-journey ticket (€13). Other cities rely mainly on buses operated by local companies like Rodoviária and Rede Expressos.
Always validate your ticket on the machine when boarding buses and trams—inspectors check frequently and unvalidated tickets incur €50 fines.

items.label: Tourist passes exist
items.text: Lisbon offers a 24/48/72-hour tourist pass (€10/€20/€27) covering metro, tram, bus and even some trains; worth it if you're sightseeing heavily.

items.label: Use Uber or Beat
items.text: Uber and Beat (formerly Taxify) operate in Lisbon, Porto and some coastal cities; they're cheaper and safer than hailing random taxis, with transparent pricing and card payment built in.
Unlicensed taxis and unofficial drivers at airports prey on tourists. Use only white official taxis with meters, or pre-book via Uber; expect €15–25 from Lisbon airport to city center.

items.label: Request a receipt
items.text: Always ask for a printed receipt (recibo) if paying cash, and note the taxi number—it protects you if there's a dispute.

items.label: Night surcharge applies
items.text: After midnight, taxis add a €1.60 surcharge and charge higher rates per km; Uber and Beat show surges in-app so you know the cost upfront.

You must have an International Driving Permit with your license, carry documents (registration, insurance), two warning triangles, high-visibility vests, and a breathalyzer kit—police fine heavily for any missing item.

items.label: Speed limits & fines
items.text: Speed limits are 50 km/h in towns, 90 km/h on regional roads and 120 km/h on motorways; speeding fines start at €120 and can reach €600 for excess over 40 km/h, enforced by radar and cameras.
Motorways (A1, A2, A6, A8) require a toll card (Via Verde) or electronic payment; rental cars often have this, but check. Missing tolls incur €25+ penalties plus interest.

items.label: Park carefully in cities
items.text: Lisbon and Porto have paid parking zones (blue lines); use the ParkWhiz or Wejo apps or buy a ticket from machines. Towed cars cost €150+ to retrieve.

items.label: Fuel is pricier than elsewhere
items.text: Petrol runs €1.50–1.70/liter; fill up at highway rest stops or Galp/BP stations rather than tourist zones. Credit cards are widely accepted.

items.label: Lisbon airport is main hub
items.text: Cristiano Ronaldo International (Humberto Delgado) is 7 km north; the red line metro (Linha Vermelha) reaches the city center in 23 minutes for €1.50–2.00, or a taxi costs €15–25.

items.label: Porto and Faro alternatives
items.text: Porto airport is 13 km north (metro Line A, 30 min, €2.00) and Faro is 9 km west (bus 16, 30 min, €3.15). Both are less chaotic than Lisbon.
Airport taxis are not metered; agree on the price beforehand or use Uber, which typically shows €20–30 to city center and protects you from overcharging.

items.label: Rental car desks at all airports
items.text: Major companies (Hertz, Europcar, Budget) are present; book ahead online for cheaper rates, and always decline the rental company's insurance if your personal policy covers it.

items.label: Lisbon river crossings
items.text: Transtejo operates cheap ferries across the Tagus; the Lisbon–Cacilhas crossing (€1.30–2.00) is scenic and connects to the south bank. Buy tickets at the terminal or load a Viva Viagem card.

items.label: Douro River cruises in Porto
items.text: Tourist ferries run under the famous bridges and along the Douro; these are more sightseeing than transit, costing €5–15 depending on route length.

items.label: No major long-distance ferries
items.text: Portugal has no regular car or passenger ferries to Spain or other countries; day trips require buses or driving over land borders.

items.label: Viva Viagem card (Lisbon)
items.text: Buy this reloadable card at metro stations or the airport for €0.50, then load journeys or day passes; it works on all metro, tram and bus networks and is far cheaper than single tickets (€0.64–2.00 per ride).

items.label: Andante card (Porto)
items.text: Similar to Viva Viagem, this card costs €0.60 and you load zones/journeys; a 10-journey ticket is €13 and covers metro and buses across Porto and nearby towns.

items.label: Portugal Rail Pass
items.text: If you're doing multi-city train trips, CP's Portugal Rail Pass (3–15 days, €50–200) offers unlimited national rail travel; buy it before arrival or at major stations.
Tourist passes and cards are non-refundable once loaded; calculate your usage carefully and buy only what you'll use, or risk losing the balance.

items.label: Buy a local SIM card
items.text: MEO, Vodafone and NOS are the three main carriers. A basic SIM costs €10–20 with 10–20 GB data and unlimited calls/texts; buy at airports, supermarkets or train stations and activate in minutes.

items.label: eSIM option
items.text: If your phone supports eSIM, buy a Portuguese plan from Airalo or your home carrier's roaming app; it's instant and costs €5–15 for 5–10 GB, avoiding physical SIM hassles.

items.label: WiFi in cities is good
items.text: Most cafés, hotels and metro stations in Lisbon, Porto and coastal towns offer free WiFi; telcos also provide free WiFi hotspots at major hubs, though speeds vary.

items.label: EU roaming works
items.text: If you're from the EU, your home SIM's roaming is included at domestic rates; US/other international users should buy a local SIM as roaming fees are steep (€2–5/MB on some carriers).
